text.title: Orchestration and Ingestion Patterns
text.summary: Airflow vs Dagster vs Prefect selection, DAG design rules (idempotency, granularity, backfill), ingestion patterns (full/incremental/CDC/streaming), and schema registry.

section.title: Orchestration Tool Selection

rule.statement: Choose the orchestrator based on team size and whether asset lineage or task scheduling is the primary concern.
rule.rationale: Airflow's operator-first model is mature for time-based scheduling but has a steep learning curve. Dagster's asset-first model makes lineage and testing first-class, which is more maintainable for data product teams. Prefect offers the fastest onboarding for small teams.

list.item: Airflow: large-scale production, 600+ operators, complex time-based scheduling, enterprise with dedicated platform team.
Dagster: asset-centric approach, strong testing (pytest integration), data lineage critical, modern developer experience priority.
Prefect: small-to-mid team, rapid development, dynamic event-driven workflows, minimal infrastructure overhead.

section.reference: DAG skeleton with exponential backoff retry, SLA miss callback, and idempotent task pattern

rule.statement: Design every task to be idempotent: running it twice with the same inputs must produce the same outputs without side effects.
rule.rationale: Pipeline failures trigger retries. Non-idempotent tasks create duplicates or corrupt state on retry. Idempotency is the foundation of reliable pipeline operations.

rule.statement: Do not make tasks too granular (one row per task) or too coarse (entire pipeline in one task); size tasks to represent one logical transformation step.
rule.rationale: Too granular: scheduler overhead dominates. Too coarse: partial failure requires full re-run. One logical step per task enables precise failure isolation and minimal re-work.

antipattern.description: Using Python operators with external API calls that have no retry or timeout configuration.
antipattern.reason: Network failures cause permanent task failure without retry. Uncapped retries without backoff hammer the API and trigger rate limiting.

list.item: Full Load: initial data load, small dimension tables, sources without reliable change tracking. Truncate and reload.
Incremental Load: large fact tables, event/transaction data. Use watermark (updated_at column or sequence ID) to load only new/changed rows.
CDC (Change Data Capture): near-real-time replication capturing inserts, updates, and deletes. Tools: Debezium, Fivetran. Use when delete propagation is required.
Event Streaming: real-time event ingestion via Kafka producers. Configure topic partitioning on the natural partition key (user_id, tenant_id).

rule.statement: Implement a schema registry for all streaming ingestion pipelines and enforce backward-compatible schema evolution.
rule.rationale: Schema drift (source adds, removes, or changes a field) breaks downstream consumers. A registry enforces compatibility rules and prevents silent data corruption at the consumer.
